allopack.title: Food Webs — Who Eats Whom, and What Happens Next
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: NGSS 5-LS2-1 (develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment); NGSS 5-PS3-1 (use models to describe that energy in animals' food was once energy from the sun); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.5.3 (explain relationships between concepts in a scientific text)

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

In 1958, people tried to help a forest by removing one animal they thought was a pest. The forest fell apart. Today you learn why pulling one thread moves the whole web.

1. Read **Pull One Thread** — the wolf story at the end is true.
2. Learn the words in the **Glossary**, then win the **Bingo** round.
3. Study the **Web Roles** chart — every living thing has a job.
4. Sort the organisms in **What's Your Job?**
5. Take the quiz — question 4 asks you to predict a chain reaction.
6. Use the **Sentence Frames** to explain a food web in your own words.

Big idea to carry: in an ecosystem there is no such thing as doing only one thing. 🐺🌲

objectives.label: I named three living things from my own neighborhood and their roles

history.title: Pull One Thread
history.data:
## Pull One Thread

Every living thing has to get energy from somewhere. Follow that energy far enough back and it always ends in the same place: sunlight.

**Producers** make their own food. Plants, algae, and some bacteria capture sunlight and build sugar. Everything else on Earth is a customer.

**Consumers** eat other living things. Herbivores like deer and grasshoppers eat producers. Carnivores like hawks and wolves eat other consumers. Omnivores like bears and humans do both. Each step is a **trophic level** — a rung on the energy ladder.

**Decomposers** get the least attention and do the most essential work. Fungi, bacteria, and worms break down dead plants, dead animals, and waste. That releases nutrients back into the soil, where producers can use them again.

Without decomposers, an ecosystem would bury itself in its own dead material. The nutrients would never get back into circulation, and everything would starve.

A **food chain** is one straight path: grass → grasshopper → mouse → hawk. Real nature almost never works that neatly. A hawk eats mice, snakes, and rabbits. A mouse eats seeds, insects, and berries. When you draw all those connections at once, you get a **food web**, and a web behaves very differently from a chain.

Here is the part worth remembering: energy leaks. Only about **ten percent** of the energy at one level makes it to the next. The rest gets spent on moving, breathing, and staying warm. Much of it escapes as heat.

That one fact explains a lot. A meadow can support thousands of grasshoppers, hundreds of mice, and only a couple of hawks. Big predators are rare because the energy math will not support more of them.

Now the thread-pulling. Wolves were hunted out of Yellowstone by the 1920s. Without them, elk populations grew and grazed heavily along rivers, stripping young willows and aspens. Beavers lost their building material. Songbirds lost nesting cover. Riverbanks eroded without roots holding the soil.

Wolves were reintroduced in 1995, and much of that reversed. Willows recovered in places. Beavers returned. Some riverbanks stabilized.

Scientists still argue about how much of this the wolves caused directly. Weather and bear populations mattered too. Ecosystems are complicated, and honest scientists debate the details.

But the core lesson is not in dispute: **you cannot remove one species and change only one thing.**

data.def: A living thing that makes its own food from sunlight, like a plant.
A living thing that eats other living things for energy.
A living thing that breaks down dead material and returns nutrients to the soil.
An animal that eats only plants.
An animal that eats other animals.
An animal that eats both plants and animals.
One single path showing who eats whom.
All the feeding connections in an ecosystem drawn together.
All the living and nonliving things in an area, interacting.
What living things need to move, grow, and stay alive — originally from the Sun.
A substance living things need to grow, recycled by decomposers.
All the members of one species living in an area.

sections.label: PRODUCERS
sections.iconPrompt: a green leafy plant under the sun

sections.label: CONSUMERS
sections.iconPrompt: a deer and a hawk facing each other

sections.label: DECOMPOSERS
bullets: Break down dead things and waste
Send nutrients back to the soil
sections.iconPrompt: mushrooms growing on a fallen log

sections.label: THE 10% RULE
bullets: Only ~10% of energy moves up a level
The rest is used up or lost as heat
This is why top predators are rare

sections.label: WEBS, NOT CHAINS
bullets: Remove one species and many change
There is no doing only one thing

history.title: What's Your Job?

items.content: Mushroom growing on a dead log
Black bear eating berries and fish
Bacteria breaking down a dead deer
Venus flytrap (a plant that also traps insects)

history.meta: 3 roles • 10 organisms • the flytrap is the argument-starter

history.title: Systems Check

questions.question: Trace the energy in a hawk's body far enough back. Where did it originally come from?
options: The Sun
The soil
The hawk's own body heat
Water in the ecosystem

questions.question: Why does an ecosystem need decomposers?
options: They return nutrients from dead material back to the soil for producers
They keep predator populations from growing too large
They make food from sunlight when plants cannot
They provide most of the energy for consumers
questions.correctAnswer: They return nutrients from dead material back to the soil for producers

questions.question: Why are there far fewer hawks than grasshoppers in a meadow?
options: Only about 10% of energy passes to each higher level
Hawks reproduce only once every ten years
Grasshoppers are better at hiding than hawks
Hawks eat only plants, which are scarce
questions.correctAnswer: Only about 10% of energy passes to each higher level

questions.question: A disease wipes out most of the mice in a meadow. Predict the most likely chain reaction.
options: Hawk numbers drop and seed-eating insects may increase
Nothing else changes, since only mice were affected
Grass immediately dies without mice to eat it
Decomposers disappear because they only eat mice
questions.correctAnswer: Hawk numbers drop and seed-eating insects may increase

questions.question: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web?
options: A chain shows one path; a web shows all the connections at once
A chain is for land and a web is for water
A web only includes producers and decomposers
There is no difference — they are two names for one thing
questions.correctAnswer: A chain shows one path; a web shows all the connections at once

questions.question: Explain what happened in Yellowstone after wolves were removed, and what scientists still debate about it.
questions.expectedAnswer: Without wolves, elk grazed heavily along rivers and stripped young willows and aspens, which affected beavers, songbirds, and riverbank erosion. After wolves returned in 1995 much of this reversed. Scientists still debate how much of the change wolves caused directly versus other factors like weather and bears.

questions.question: Is a Venus flytrap a producer or a consumer? Defend your answer.
questions.expectedAnswer: It is a producer, because it makes its own food by photosynthesis. It traps insects for extra nutrients like nitrogen in poor soil, not for energy — so it feeds like a plant and supplements like a consumer.

reflections.text: Pick one animal near where you live. What eats it, and what does it eat?
If humans disappeared from your local ecosystem tomorrow, name two things that would change.

history.meta: 5 MCQ + 2 short answer • prediction + honest debate

items.text: If ____ disappeared from this ecosystem, then ____, and then ____.
A food web is different from a food chain because ____.

data.rubric:
| Criteria | 1 — Starting | 3 — Getting There | 5 — Ecologist |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roles | Names organisms only | Uses producer/consumer/decomposer | Uses all roles precisely, including trophic levels |
| Energy | Does not mention energy | Says energy moves | Explains energy loss and why predators are rare |
| Systems thinking | One-step effect | Two-step chain | Traces effects through 3+ connections in a web |
